response:
Based on what I recall about your situation, you're a self-employed UX consultant filing Schedule C in Austin, TX — which puts you in a strong position to deduct both a portion of rent and home internet as legitimate business expenses.
Since these are tax-related deductions, I'll explain the concepts educationally. Per CFP Board standards, consult a qualified tax advisor or CPA for advice specific to your situation before making decisions.
Home office deduction (rent)
As a Schedule C filer, you can deduct the business-use percentage of your rent — but only if your home office meets two IRS tests:
Exclusive use: The space must be used only for business — no personal use allowed. A dedicated room or clearly defined area works; a kitchen table does not.
Regular use: You must use it consistently and on an ongoing basis, not just occasionally.
Principal place of business: Since you're a UX consultant working from home with no other fixed office, your home is almost certainly your principal place of business for administrative work (client communication, invoicing, project management) — which satisfies this test.
How the deduction is calculated:
You choose one of two methods each year:
Simplified method: $5 per square foot of qualifying office space, up to 300 sq ft — maximum $1,500/year. Simple, no depreciation, no carryforward.
Regular (actual expense) method: Divide your office square footage by your total home square footage to get your business-use percentage, then apply that percentage to your rent, utilities, insurance, and other indirect expenses. For example, if your office is 15% of your home and your annual rent is $24,000, you could potentially deduct $3,600 in rent alone through this method.
The regular method typically yields a higher deduction if your rent is significant — which is common in Austin.
Home internet
This one is clean and straightforward for self-employed consultants. You can deduct the business-use portion of your monthly internet bill as either a direct Schedule C expense (under "utilities" or "office expense") or as an indirect home office expense if you use the actual expense method.
The key is documenting your business-use percentage with a reasonable method:
Time-based approach: Track a representative month — log hours used for business (client calls, design work, research, file uploads) versus total hours online. If 40% of your internet use is for business, deduct 40% of your bill year-round.
Avoid claiming 100% unless you have a completely separate business-only internet line. Mixed personal/business use requires honest allocation.
Keep your internet bills and a usage log for at least 3–7 years in case of an audit.
Practical next steps
Measure your office square footage and compare both methods to see which gives you the larger deduction.
Start a simple log of your internet business use this month as your baseline.
Your Ally Tax Reserve savings account is a smart setup for self-employment — make sure your quarterly estimated payments are accounting for the full Schedule C income after these deductions, which will meaningfully reduce your self-employment tax base as well.
Would you like help thinking through which home office method — simplified or actual expenses — is likely to work better given your rent level in Austin?
Note: Calculations, potential savings, and eligibility determinations are estimations for illustrative purposes only. Tax laws change frequently. Consult a qualified tax professional before making decisions.
